ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic milk tea entry (FOODON_03311737); no specific entry exists for honey oat green milk tea. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for honey oat green milk tea variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 is the closest available concept for milk tea; no specific code for honey oat green milk tea exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of honey oat green milk tea, approximately 500 ml (500 g), as typically served at a Taiwanese-style tea shop in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea with sugar, ready-to-drink; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA entries for honey (NDB 19296) and oats (NDB 08120) to model honey-oat variant
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB Singapore entry exists for honey oat green milk tea. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central entry for milk tea / tea with milk (approx. 40–50 kcal/100 ml base), (2) honey contribution (~3 g sugar per 100 ml at typical 6% honey addition), and (3) oat syrup/flakes contribution (~2 g carbohydrate per 100 ml at typical 4% oat addition). The resulting composite per-100 g profile reflects a sweetened green milk tea with moderate sugar and low fat, consistent with commercially prepared Taiwanese-style milk teas. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across outlets.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea and honey-containing beverages — analogous estimate based on: (1) sweetened milk tea GI ~55 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (2) honey GI ~55 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); (3) oat-based additions lower GI marginally
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journals (Diabetes Care; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey oat green milk tea. GI of 55 is estimated from: sweetened milk tea GI ~55 (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008; University of Sydney GI Database entry for tea with milk and sugar); honey GI ~55 (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002); oat beta-glucan content is low at typical beverage concentrations and does not materially lower GI. Composite GI estimated at 55 (low). GL per 500 g serving = (55 × 59.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 32.45.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~140 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Honey oat green milk tea contains approximately 140 mg sodium per standard 500 ml serving, which is low; however, individuals with hypertension should be mindful of total daily sugar intake from sweetened beverages, as excess sugar can contribute to weight gain and elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content and low glycemic index (~55) with high glycemic load per serving (~32.45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 47.5 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~32.45 per 500 ml serving, honey oat green milk tea poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed only occasionally, in reduced portions, or substituted with an unsweetened or reduced-sugar version.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high added sugar per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sweetened milk tea beverages like honey oat green milk tea contributes to elevated added sugar intake (~47.5 g per 500 ml serving), which is associated with increased triglyceride levels and cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and opt for reduced-sugar or unsweetened alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Oat Green Milk Tea (松针燕麦奶蜜茶) is a Taiwanese-inspired specialty milk tea beverage sold in Singapore by CHICHA San Chen, a premium tea chain. It is prepared by brewing high-quality green tea (often described as 'pine needle' green tea, referencing the needle-shaped tea leaves), then blending it with fresh milk or a dairy-based creamer, natural honey for sweetness, and oat flakes or oat syrup for a subtle nutty, creamy texture. The drink is typically served chilled over ice in a medium or large cup. The flavour profile is delicately sweet and grassy from the green tea, with a mild floral honey note and a smooth, slightly thick mouthfeel contributed by the oat component. The oat addition provides a small amount of beta-glucan fibre, though at beverage concentrations the fibre contribution is modest. Nutritionally, a standard 500 ml serving delivers approximately 310 kcal, 59 g of carbohydrate (predominantly from honey and added sugar), 7 g of protein, and 6 g of fat. The glycemic load per serving is high (~32.45), making this beverage a significant source of rapidly absorbed carbohydrates. It is not suitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or those on calorie-restricted diets. The drink is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains milk and gluten (from oats). It is representative of the premium Taiwanese tea shop category that has expanded widely across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for honey oat green milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA milk tea, honey, and oat entries. GI is estimated by analogy from peer-reviewed sweetened milk tea and honey GI data. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across outlets and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite (milk tea base + honey + oat syrup contributions) due to absence of an exact approved-source entry. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation — no hydrogenated fats in standard milk tea preparation). 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — green tea contains negligible vitamin C after brewing and dilution with milk; confirmed trace/negligible per USDA). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source for this beverage category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source for this beverage category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source for this beverage category. 7. GI set to 55 (low) by analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002). 8. GL per 500 g serving calculated as (55 × 59.0) / 100 = 32.45. 9. Food name in input JSON includes 'NEW' as a promotional tag; canonical English name normalised to 'Honey Oat Green Milk Tea'. 10. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Chinese-cuisine beverage sold in Singapore. 11. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact ontology entry exists for this specific beverage.
